p_question-4875.ja: 以下は 、 新聞のコラムである 。 プリプリ海老 昔に比べてレストランのメニューに修飾語が増えたと感じる。「プリプリ海老の○○パスタ」「とろ〜りチーズ入りのハンバーグ」「XX県産のやわらか若鶏の△△」といった感じだ。付加価値や希少価値をアピールして、商品の差別化を図りたいのだろう。 これらの修飾語は[41]食欲を刺激し期待感を高める。でもよくよく考えると、差別化の中身は実にあいまいだ。プリプリ海老の定義は？チーズはみな、とろ〜りするのでは？XX県産が品質保証になるのか？ 実質的な中身を伴った新商品の開発が困難になっている高度消費社会では、見せかけの新しさで消費を刺激しようとする傾向が強まる。商品のパッケージを新しくしたり、商品名の変更が頻繁に行われたりするのも[42]。消費社会とは、商品の差別化戦略によって中身の空虚な（注1）ネーミング（注2）が氾濫し、消費者がそれに踊らされる世界ではないのか。 そのような社会は、私たちの言語感覚[43]変えてしまう可能性がある。商品の差別化競争に終わりがないなら、修飾語こそが肝要になる。つまり、修飾語を伴った呼称がデフォルト（注3）となり、裸の名詞が空虚に感じられるようになるかもしれない。 何十年かすれば、生物としての海老という記号は空洞化して、おいしそうな食材としての修飾語が不可欠な要素となり、「海老」は「プリプリ海老」、「海老のチリソース」は「プリプリ海老のチリソース」と呼ばれているのだろうか。 私たちは、増殖する見せかけの新しさに囲まれながら、空虚な記号と戯れ続ける（注4）しかないのか？メニューに並ぶ修飾語は、消費社会の遠い未来からの[44]。 （ 注 1 ） 空虚な ： 実質的な意味がない（ 注 2 ） ネーミング ： 名前を付けること（ 注 3 ） デフォルト ： 標準（ 注 4 ） 戯れ続ける ： ここでは 、 付き合い続ける
p_question-4875.en: The following is a newspaper column. “Plump, springy” shrimp Compared with the past, I feel that menus at restaurants have more modifiers. Things like “○○ pasta with plump, springy shrimp,” “hamburg steak stuffed with gooey cheese,” and “△△ made with tender young chicken from XX Prefecture.” They’re probably trying to emphasize added or rare value and differentiate their products. These modifiers [41] stimulate the appetite and heighten expectations. But on reflection, what they contain as differentiation is awfully vague. What is the definition of “plump, springy shrimp”? Doesn’t all cheese turn gooey? Does being produced in XX Prefecture guarantee quality? In an advanced consumer society where it has become difficult to develop truly substantive new products, there is a growing tendency to spur consumption with superficial newness. The frequent renewal of packaging and changes of product names are also [42]. Isn’t a consumer society one in which empty (Note 1) namings (Note 2), born of differentiation strategies, flood the market and consumers are made to dance to them? Such a society may [43] change our sense of language. If the competition to differentiate never ends, modifiers become essential. In other words, names with modifiers could become the default (Note 3), and bare nouns might start to feel empty. In a few decades, might the sign “shrimp” as a living creature become hollow, and modifiers that make it sound tasty become indispensable elements—so that “shrimp” is called “plump, springy shrimp,” and “shrimp in chili sauce” becomes “chili sauce with plump, springy shrimp”? Surrounded by proliferating superficial newness, are we doomed to keep toying (Note 4) with empty signs? The modifiers lined up on menus are a [44] from the distant future of the consumer society. (Note 1) empty: lacking substantive meaning(Note 2) naming: the act of giving a name(Note 3) default: standard(Note 4) keep toying with: here, keep engaging with

p_question-4880.ja: 「嫌い」はネガティブな（注）感情で、まさに嫌悪感をもってしまいがちですが、自分の中に芽生えてきた大切な感情であり、大事な機能でもあります、何より根絶できません。 「嫌い」はとてもパワーを必要とする感情ですから、それだけに反動も大きい。振り回されないための第一歩は、嫌いを認めることです。ネガティブな感情でも、もつことは自然なことであり、生きる上でメリットになる、そして周囲の人にもメリットがあると捉えてみてください。 （注）ネガティブな：否定的
p_question-4880.en: “Dislike” is a negative (Note) emotion, and we tend to feel aversion toward it; yet it is a valuable feeling that sprouts within us and a vital function—and above all, it cannot be eradicated. Because “dislike” is an emotion that consumes a lot of energy, the rebound is correspondingly large. The first step to not being tossed around by it is to acknowledge your dislike. Even negative emotions are natural to have; try to see them as beneficial for living—and also beneficial for those around you. (Note) negative: unfavorable

p_question-4881.ja: （２） 安く売るべきか、品質にこだわるべきか。事はそんなに単純ではない。どんなに良い商品であったとしても、できるだけ価格を抑えるための努力は必要だ。品質にこだわる戦略というのは、品質さえ良ければどんなに高くても買ってもらえるという短絡的な戦略ではない。 できるだけ価格を抑える努力はしているけれども、それでも品質にこだわるとこれ以上は安くできない。他社の商品よりも高いのだけれども、それだけの価値はある。だからその差額を許容してほしい。そう訴えることが、品質にこだわった正しい戦略である。
p_question-4881.en: (2) Should we sell cheaply, or insist on quality? The matter isn’t that simple. No matter how good a product is, you still need to make every effort to keep the price down. A strategy that emphasizes quality is not a simplistic one that assumes people will buy at any price as long as quality is good. It means: we are trying to hold prices down as much as possible, but if we insist on quality we can’t make it any cheaper. Our product costs more than others’, but it’s worth that much. So please accept the difference. Making that case is the correct strategy when you are committed to quality.

p_question-4882.ja: （３） 時には、自らに「素朴な質問」を投げかけてみてはどうだろうか。子どもが親に尋ねるような「素朴な質問」というものは、意外にも人生や生きることの本質にかかわることであり、人生論や哲学の課題であることが少なくない。そうした質問を真正面から投げかけられたとき、はっと何か気づかされるところがあって、自分の日常を反省するような反応を示さないとしたら、その人の感性は相当に（注）枯れていると考えたほうがよい。 （注）相当に：かなり
p_question-4882.en: (3) At times, why not pose “simple questions” to yourself? The “simple questions” a child asks a parent often turn out, unexpectedly, to concern the essence of life and living, and are frequently topics for life philosophy or philosophy proper. If, when faced head-on with such a question, a person feels no jolt of realization and shows no response that makes them reflect on their daily life, it’s best to assume that their sensibility is rather (Note) withered. (Note) rather: considerably

p_question-4883.ja: （４） 科学とは、知れば知るほどわからないことが増えてくるものである。自分は何も知らなかった思い知らされるのが科学者の日常と言える。つまり、科学者は研究を極めれば極めるほど謙虚になる。自分の無知さを知って謙虚になちざるをえないのだ。その観点から言えば、知ったかぶりをする（注）科学者はもはや研究をストップしており、それまでに得た知識を誇ってているに過ぎないと言うことができる。もはや過去の人で、あり、その知識は時代遅れになっている可能性が高いのだ。 （注）知ったかぶりをする：知っているふりをする
p_question-4883.en: (4) Science is something that, the more you learn, the more you realize how much you don’t know. It could be said that a scientist’s daily life is being made aware of how ignorant one was. In other words, the more a scientist pursues research to its utmost, the more humble they become. Knowing one’s own ignorance, one cannot help but be humble. From that perspective, a (Note) scientist who pretends to know has in effect already stopped doing research and is merely boasting of knowledge acquired up to that point. Such a person already belongs to the past, and the likelihood is high that their knowledge is outdated. (Note) to pretend to know: to act as if one knows

p_question-4884.ja: （１） ともすると私たち大人は、自分たちの子ども時代の遊びはよくて、今の子どもの遊びは好ましくないと考えがちです。（中略） 今はダメで昔はよかったというような感覚をもって、今の子どもの遊びについて考えると、とんでもないことになります。子どもたちが嬉々として（注）遊んでいるものを取り上げて、今の時代ではおもしろくないような遊びを、無理に押しつけることになってしまいます。そうなると遊びが遊び でなくなってしまいかねません。 また、私たちが考える好ましい遊び環境となると、なぜかとても整理された空間になりがちです。たとえば画一化された公園を見ると、ムダーつなく機能を重視したオフィスを連想させさえします。限られたスペースで、しかも安全性や管理の問題を考えればそうならざるをえないのかもしれませんが、それでは子どもが遊びたいと思わなくなるのです。 子どもたちの遊び空間には、もしかしたら不自由さや不便さ、大人がムダだと思えるようなものも必要かもしれません。配慮の行き届きすぎた場所は、遊園地という商業施設は別として、子どもたちが自由に遊べる環境としては好ましくないのではないでしょうか。 子どもたちの遊び環境について考えるときに大切なのは、私たち大人の側にあるそのような先入観です。自分は子どものころにこんな遊びをした。今の子どもはそれをしていない。だから今の子どもは遊んでいない。こんな単純な考え方では、決して子どものためになる遊び環境など考えることはできません。 （注）嬉々として：喜んで
p_question-4884.en: (1) We adults tend to think that the play we did in our own childhood was good, while today’s children’s play is undesirable. (Omitted.) If we think about children’s play with the feeling that “what’s now is bad, what was in the past was good,” we end up in a terrible place. We take away what children are happily (Note) playing and forcibly impose play that isn’t interesting in today’s era. In that case, play may cease to be play at all. Also, when we imagine a desirable play environment, it somehow tends to become a very tidy space. For example, a standardized park even makes one think of an office that overemphasizes function without waste. Given limited space and concerns about safety and management, perhaps it can’t be helped—but that makes children not want to play there. Children’s play spaces may actually need inconveniences and inconveniences—things adults might deem wasteful. Over-scrupulously designed places—amusement parks as commercial facilities aside—are not necessarily desirable as environments where children can play freely. What matters when considering children’s play environments are the preconceptions on our adult side. “I played like this when I was a child. Children today don’t do that. Therefore children today don’t really play.” With such a simplistic way of thinking, we can never design play environments that are truly good for children. (Note) happily: with delight

p_question-4886.ja: （２） 私たち人間は、いろいろなものを食べる雑食性動物です。それに対し、基本的にパンダはササだけを食べ、コアラ（注1）はユーカリの葉だけを食べます。動物の生き残り戦略を考えると、雑食性動物の方が、慣れ親しんだ食べものが入手困難な状況になったとしても、それ以外の食べられるものへと嗜好をシフトすることによって飢餓を脱し、生存する確率を高めることができます。雑食性動物は環境適応性に優れた生きものといえます。 しかしその一方で、新たに見つけ出した食べものが毒性をもっていたり、栄養バランスが悪いものであれば、健康を損ね、最悪死に至る可能性があります。そのため、雑食性動物は、新しい食べものを食べるときには、必ずそのリスクと対峙（注2）しなければなりません。 すなわち雑食性動物は、食べたことのないものを食べるのを躊躇する「食物新奇性恐怖」と、積極的に食べようとする「食物新奇性嗜好」という相矛盾（注3）する行動傾向を、生まれながらに合わせもっているといえます。（中略） 雑食性動物がもつ食べものの新奇性恐怖と新奇性嗜好のジレンマを解消してきたもののひとつが、人の「調理」という行為です。食べたことのない食材、たとえば昆虫をそのままの"原型"で出されると、拒否感を抱く人が多いですが、粉にしてチップスなど、見慣れたお菓子のかたちで提供されれば、食べる人は確実に増えます。さらに慣れ親しんだ調味料で味付けしたものなどであれば、「あっ、意外とおいしい」と好評価を得るかもしれません。この調理という操作が、新奇なものを食べるという恐怖を緩和させるのに一役買って（注4）います。 （注1）コアラ：動物の一種 （注2）対峙する：ここでは、向き合う （注3）相～：互いに～ （注4）一役買う：ここでは、役立つ
p_question-4886.en: (2) Humans are omnivores that eat many kinds of things. In contrast, pandas basically eat only bamboo grass, and koalas (Note 1) eat only eucalyptus leaves. Considering animals’ survival strategies, omnivores can raise their probability of survival by shifting their preferences to other edible items when familiar foods become hard to obtain. In that sense, omnivores are highly adaptable to their environment. On the other hand, if newly discovered foods are toxic or nutritionally unbalanced, health may suffer and, in the worst case, death may result. Therefore, when omnivores eat something new, they must inevitably confront that risk (Note 2). That is, omnivores are born with two mutually contradictory (Note 3) behavioral tendencies: “food neophobia,” a hesitation to eat unfamiliar items, and “food neophilia,” an eagerness to try them. (Omitted.) One thing that has resolved this dilemma between fear and attraction to new foods is the human act of “cooking.” If unfamiliar ingredients—say, insects—are served in their original form, many people feel rejection; but if they are milled into powder and offered as chips or other familiar snacks, the number of people who will eat them surely increases. Seasoning them with familiar flavors may even earn the response, “Oh, this is surprisingly tasty.” This operation we call cooking plays a part (Note 4) in easing the fear of eating the novel. (Note 1) koala: a kind of animal (Note 2) confront: here, face (Note 3) mutually ~: reciprocally ~ (Note 4) play a part: here, be helpful

p_question-4888.ja: （３） 私は、自分の研究をおもしろいと思えるのと同じ程度に、他人の研究をおもしろいと思えるかどうかが、研究者に向いているか否かの判断の基準であると思っている。いくらいいデータを出す人であっても、他人のデータを自分の仕事と同じだけの熱量を持っておもしろがれなければ、研究者としてははっきり不向きであると思わざるを得ない。学者としては失格であろう。 これがもっとも端的にあらわれるのが、研究発表の際に見られる質問の量である。 （中略） 発表された内容を、当事者として自分ならこういうアイデアで実験をし、結果をこう解釈することもできる、明確な結論を得るためにはこの部分に不備があり、次にはこんな実験を計画すれば、もっとはっきりした結論に到達することができるのではないか、などなど、考え始めれば、おのずから尋ねたいことは次から次へ出てくるはずなのである。 私はこれを「能動的に聞く」と言っている。人の話は、能動的に聞いてこそ、自らの身につくものである。話された内容をただひたすら覚えようとしたり、吸収しようとしているだけでは、却ってその知識は自分のものとならない。 「能動的に聞く」とは、話された内容を、自らのこれまでの知の体系のなかに位置づけることであり、位置づけるためには、聞きつつ常に自分の知の体系を確認し、照合する作業を伴うはずである。外部からインプットされてくる内容と、既存の自らの知識の箱とのあいだに軋轢が生じるのは当然であり、その軋轢こそが質問を促す力になる筈なのだ。
p_question-4888.en: (3) I believe that a criterion for whether one is suited to be a researcher is whether one can find other people’s research as interesting as one’s own. No matter how good the data someone produces, if they cannot enjoy others’ data with the same intensity as their own work, I can only conclude that they are clearly unsuited as a researcher—indeed, unqualified as a scholar. This shows most plainly in the amount of questioning observed at research presentations. (Omitted.) Once you begin thinking as a party concerned—if it were me, I would run experiments with this idea, the results could be interpreted like this, this part is insufficient to reach a clear conclusion, next we might plan such-and-such an experiment to arrive at a firmer conclusion—then the questions you want to ask should naturally come one after another. I call this “listening actively.” Only by listening actively do other people’s talks truly become part of oneself. If you merely try to memorize or absorb what is said, paradoxically that knowledge will not become your own. “Listening actively” means situating what is said within your existing system of knowledge; to do so, you must, as you listen, continually check and compare against your own system. Friction between the incoming content and your existing boxes of knowledge is natural, and that very friction becomes the force that prompts questions.

p_question-4890.ja: （４） 企業Aと企業Bがあり、ある契約をそのどちらと取り交わすべきか考えるときに、A社は資本金1000万円、B社は資本金1億円であるという情報は、おそらく重要な情報の一つであると想定されるが、すべてではない。従業員数、年商（注）、営業継続年数、支社数・支店数、などなどのうちから、意思決定者が、意思決定の方針に基づいて重要な情報を取捨選択しなければならないだろう。そのとき、ことさらにいくつかの情報に力点を置く報告書があげられたときに、意思決定者（経営者）はそこに存在する「意図」を充分に嗅ぎ取らなくてはならない。つまり、汚染されている可能性があるということである。ここで、報告された情報がすべて確実な事実であったとしても汚染は生じている可能性があるという点に注意が必要である。 したがって、情報汚染とは、ある情報に、本来想定しているもの以外の何かが付随していて、それによってその情報の理解が歪むことをいう。その何かとは、その情報をもたらした側の意図である。この世界に「データのみで構成されていて、そこに意図の混入が認められない」という意味での無色透明な情報など存在しない。どのような情報であっても、意図という色で染められている。そして、重要なのはそれが必ずしも汚染ではないという点である。意図が混入するのは当然のことであり、すべての情報には意図が混入しているのであるから、それだけでもってそれを汚染とまでは呼べない。それが汚染となるのは、その情報に含まれる「意図」によって、その情報の本質部分である「データ」の理解が歪められるからである。 （注）年商：一年間の売り上げ額
p_question-4890.en: (4) Suppose there are Company A and Company B, and you are deciding which one to conclude a certain contract with. Information such as “A has capital of 10 million yen, B has capital of 100 million yen” is presumably one important data point, but not everything. From among employee headcount, annual sales (Note), years in operation, number of branches or offices, and so on, the decision maker must select what is important in line with the decision policy. When a report is submitted that places special emphasis on certain items, the decision maker (manager) must keenly sniff out the “intent” present there—in other words, recognize the possibility of contamination. Note that contamination may arise even when all reported information is factual. Thus, information contamination refers to a state in which something other than what is originally envisaged accompanies a piece of information and thereby distorts understanding of that information. That “something” is the intent of the party that supplied the information. In this world, there is no such thing as information that is “composed only of data and free of any admixture of intent,” in the sense of being colorless and transparent. All information is dyed with the color of intent. What matters is that this is not necessarily contamination. Since the admixture of intent is natural and present in all information, we cannot call it contamination for that reason alone. It becomes contamination when the “intent” included distorts understanding of the “data,” the essential part of the information. (Note) annual sales: total sales in one year

p_question-4892.ja: 以下は、森林を守るボランティア活動をしている人が書いた文章である。 世界的に森林や自然の維持、復元に対する関心が高まってきたとき、この気運（注1）を支えた初期の理論は、人間中心主義的な森林・自然維持論、つまり人間の生存や未来にとって森林や自然の維持は不可欠であるという理論であった。いわばそれは、人間のための森林・自然維持論であった。ところが、この考え方は、たちまち暗礁に乗り上げる（注2）。 第一に、人間のための森林・自然維持論であるかぎり、人間のための森林・自然の開発論を否定できなくなる。なぜなら、どちらも「人間のための」という共通の基盤の上に立っている以上、その違いは価値観の違いにすぎず、人間の価値観を統一することなどできないばかりでなく、すべきでもないからである。 第二に、「人間のための」といっても、その内容はさまざまである。農山村に暮らす人びとと都市の市民とでは、その感じ方は同じではないし、たとえば同じ山村に暮らす者でも、林業経営をしている者とそうでない者とでは、考え方が異なってくる。人と森の関係は多様である以上、どうすることが人間のための森林・自然の維持なのかと問われれば、その答えはさまざまである。 第三に、「人間のための」という発想にたつかぎり、「人間のためにならない」自然は破壊してもよいということになりかねない。 こうした問題点があったために、「人間のための」論は、つまり人間中心的な森林・自然維持論は、たちまち破綻してしまった。自然保護思想の流れをみるなら、それに代わって生まれた理論は、自然中心主義的な考え方であった。それは、人間のためになろうとも、ならなかったとしても、自然はそれ自身のうちに生存権をもっており、その生存権は保障されなければならないというものであった。 ところが、この考え方にも問題点があった。なぜならこの理論では、原生林や原生的な自然を維持する理論としては有効でも、たとえば日本のような、長い歴史のなかで自然と人間とが相互に影響を与えながら、それぞれが暮らしてきた地域の森林・自然の問題をよく説明できないからである。 一例をあげれば、かつて農山村の人びとは、自分たちの暮らしを守るために、人家の近くに里山天然林をつくりだした。それは、徹底的に人間の手を加えた天然林である。（中略） 森林ボランティアとは、自発的に森の中で仕事をする人びとである。そうである以上、私たちは、人間中心主義的な立場に立つこともできない。 とすれば、どうすればよいのだろうか。この問いに対して、私たちは、森林・自然と人間の関係を持続的に維持していくことこそが重要だと考える。単純に人間の未来を守ることでも、森を守ることでもない。森林と人間の関係を維持することによって、森と人とが相互的な依存関係をもっている日本の森と人の営みを守っていくことである。 （注1）気運：ここでは、意識の高まり （注2）暗礁に乗り上げる：ここでは、進められなくなる
p_question-4892.en: The following was written by someone engaged in volunteer activities to protect forests. When global concern for the maintenance and restoration of forests and nature rose, the early theory supporting this momentum (Note 1) was an anthropocentric theory of conserving forests and nature—that is, a theory that maintaining forests and nature is indispensable for human survival and the human future. In short, it was conservation for humans. However, this way of thinking soon ran aground (Note 2). First, as long as it is conservation of forests and nature “for humans,” you cannot deny development of forests and nature “for humans.” Since both stand on the same footing of “for humans,” the difference is no more than a divergence of values; not only is unifying human values impossible, it is not even desirable. Second, even if we say “for humans,” the content varies. People living in farm-and-mountain villages and urban citizens do not feel the same, and even among residents of the same mountain village, those engaged in forestry and those who are not will think differently. Since the relationships between people and forests are diverse, when asked what constitutes conservation of forests and nature for humans, the answers will likewise be diverse. Third, if we proceed from the idea of “for humans,” we risk concluding that nature that is “not for humans” may be destroyed. Because of such problems, the “for humans” theory—that is, the anthropocentric conservation theory—quickly collapsed. In the flow of conservation thought, the theory that arose to replace it was ecocentrism: whether or not it benefits humans, nature itself possesses a right to exist, and that right must be guaranteed. However, this idea also had problems. While effective as a theory for preserving primeval forests and pristine nature, it does not well explain the issues of forests and nature in regions—such as Japan—where, over a long history, nature and humans have influenced each other while each has lived there. For example, in the past, people in rural mountain villages created satoyama natural forests near their homes to support their livelihoods. These were natural forests thoroughly shaped by human hands. (Omitted.) Forest volunteers are people who work of their own accord in the woods. Precisely because of that, we cannot take an anthropocentric stance. So what should we do? Our answer is that what is crucial is to sustain the relationship between forests/nature and human beings. It is not simply to protect the human future or to protect forests. By maintaining the relationship between forests and people, we safeguard the intertwined, mutually dependent way of life of Japan’s forests and its people. (Note 1) momentum: here, a rise in awareness (Note 2) run aground: here, become unable to proceed

p_question-4895.ja: （A) 勉強やスポーツなど何かに取り組んでいる時にスランプはつきもの（注）だ。いつもどおりしているのに、全く成果が上がらない。そんな時間が続くと、自分には能力が足りないのではないか、これ以上は上達しないのではないかと自信を失ってしまう。 しかし、スランプとは一時的に調子が悪くなっている状態にすぎない。誰にでも調子の良い時と悪い時があり、それは波のようにやってくる。悪い時にはそれを乗り越えようともがくのではなく、波に身を任せるようにするといい。現状をすなおに受け止め、これまでと同じことをやり続けるのだ。そうして自然に波を乗り越えれば、またやる気も自信も戻ってくる。そうすれば、自然に次にやるべきことも見えてくるものだ。 （B) スランプの状態になると、調子が良かった過去と比べてしまいます。だから、その差にショックを受けてしまって、自信を無くしてしまうわけですね。調子の波があることは普通なので、良い時と比べるのは止めましょう。 それよりも、今に集中することの方が大切です。今の自分を受け入れて、「これからどうするべきか」ということを考えてください。こういう考え方ができれば、前向きに物事を捉えることができるはずです。 いつまでも過去に捉われている人は、後悔や焦りといった感情しか出てきません。しかし、今を見ている人だと、「どうすればいいか」という生産的な考え方ができるわけですね。 （注）つきもの：当然あるもの
p_question-4895.en: (A) When you’re engaged in something—study, sports, and so on—slumps are an inevitable part of it (Note). You do things as usual, yet no results come at all. If that continues, you lose confidence, thinking you may lack ability or may never improve beyond this point. But a slump is nothing more than a temporary spell of poor form. Everyone has good and bad periods, and they come like waves. In bad times, rather than thrashing to overcome them, it’s better to let yourself ride the wave. Accept the present as it is and keep doing what you’ve been doing. If you get over the wave naturally like that, your motivation and confidence will return. Then what you should do next will come into view of its own accord. (B) When you fall into a slump, you compare yourself with the past when things were going well. That shock at the gap is why you lose confidence. Since it’s normal to have ups and downs, stop comparing with your good times. More important is to focus on the present. Accept who you are now and think about “what should I do from here?” With that mindset, you should be able to see things positively. People who stay fixated on the past only feel emotions like regret and impatience. But those who look at the present can think productively in terms of “what should I do?” (Note) tsukimono: something that naturally comes with it

p_question-4897.ja: 以下は、十代の若者に向けて書かれた文章である。 だれでも、自分が自分であることを望み、個性的であろうと願っている。そしてその一方では、自分が他のみんなと違った、自分であることを恐れている。 本当は、「個性的」であろうと思って、個性的になれるものでもない。それはたいてい、「個性的」といわれる一つの型に、自分をあてはめていることだったりする。人間にとっては、「個性的であれ」などと言われても、どうしてよいかわからないものであって、「個性的」とよばれる型を持ったほうが楽なのだ。しかしそれは、本当に個性的なわけではない。 それでも、青年期に向けて、個性的であろうと試みることは、よいことだ。かりにそれが、一つの型にすぎなくても、あるいは、個性的であろうとして挫折したり、個「生」的であることから逃避することも含めて、自分にとって個性とは、考えるのはよいことだ。それは、自分が自分であることの、あかしでもある。 そこでは、ときに悩むことがあったり、どうしたら個性的な人間になれるかと、模索することもあるかもしれない。それはじつは、「個性的」であること以上に、自分というものを意識していく、一つの過程である。そうした意味では、「個性的」であるかどうかなんて、本当はたいしたことではなくて、自分というものが確立していくことが、大事なこととも言える。 そしてじつは、個性というものは、人間のひとりひとりに備わったもので、その個性がありのままに出ていることが、本当の意味で個性的である。むしろ、本当の自分が出せないから、没個性的にもなるのだ。 もちろん、人間はまわりの社会を気にするものであって、自分をよそおうことも含めてしか、社会のなかで存在できない。無理につっぱって、自分のよそおいを捨てようとしても、捨てきれるものではない。そうした、社会とのかかわりも含めて、自分というものはある。 たいていの場合、つっぱったり、いじけたりしていては、自分の個性は発揮できない。つっぱるまいとするのが一種のつっぱりだったり、いじけまいとしてそのことにいじけたり、なんてことまで問題にしだすと、ちょっと言葉の遊びみたいになるが、ま、そうしたことまで含めて、気楽に自分であるようにしなくては、本当の意味で個性的にはなれないものだ。 そして、ありのままの自分を出すことへの不安が、「個性的」を一つの型にしたり、あるいはそうした型から逃れる道へ向かわせたりもする。 それでも結局、自分とはありのままの自分しかない。それを自覚したとき、きみは個性的なきみになる。
p_question-4897.en: The following text is addressed to teenagers. Everyone wants to be themselves and wishes to be “individual.” At the same time, everyone fears being a self that differs from everyone else. In truth, you can’t become individualistic just by deciding to be. Often that simply means forcing yourself into a mold that people call “individual.” When told to “be unique,” people don’t really know how, and it’s easier to adopt a template labeled “unique.” But that is not truly being individual. Even so, as you move into youth, attempting to be individual is a good thing. Even if it ends up being just a mold, or even if you fail at being individual or even run away from being a living “self,” it is good to think about what individuality means to you. That is proof that you are yourself. You may sometimes worry and grope for how to become an individual person. In fact, more than being “individual,” this is a process of becoming conscious of who you are. In that sense, whether you are “individual” is not really the main thing; what matters is that your self becomes established. And actually, individuality is something each person already possesses; letting that individuality appear as it is is what is truly individual. Rather, when you cannot show your real self, you end up without individuality. Of course, humans are mindful of the surrounding society, and can exist in it only by including some self-presentation. Even if you defy others and try to throw off your persona, you cannot discard it completely. Your “self” exists in relation to society, that included. In most cases, if you are bristling or sulking, your individuality won’t come out. If you make “not bristling” into another form of bristling, or “not sulking” into another way of sulking, the issue starts to become a play on words; but taking even that in stride, unless you relax and just be yourself, you cannot become individual in the true sense. And the anxiety about showing your unvarnished self can drive you to turn “individual” into a fixed mold—or send you fleeing from such molds. Even so, in the end, your self is nothing other than your unvarnished self. When you realize that, you become an individual you.

p_question-4900.ja: 星川大学 2022年度「グローバル研究活動助成」募集要項 1. 応募資格（以下の条件を満たす者） 2022年度に星川大学の大学院に在学していて、2021年度に本助成に採択されていない者 2. 対象となる研究活動（以下の①または②に当てはまる活動） ① 渡航期間10日間以上で、海外において調査を行う。 ② 海外での研究会議、シンポジウム等で研究発表を行う（渡航期間は問わない）。 3. 対象となる研究活動の実施期間 ①②ともに2022年8月1日から2023年3月31日までに行うもの 4. 助成金額等 一人あたりの助成金額の上限は20万円とします。応募は一人1件のみ可能です。助成対象の費目の詳細は別紙を参照してください。 5. 選考 書類による選考 6. スケジュール 募集期間：2022年5月10日（火）～6月10日（金） 結果通知：2022年7月中旬 7. 応募手続き 以下のA～Cを研究推進課窓口へまとめて提出してください。 A 申請書（様式1） B 推薦書（様式2） ...指導教員が作成したものを学生本人が提出すること。作成依頼は余裕をもってするようにしてください。 C 発表申し込みの写し（②の場合のみ） ...申し込みの開始が募集期間後になる場合は、準備ができ次第提出すること。 ●AとBの記入用紙は4月26日から募集期間の最終日まで研究推進課窓口で配布します。研究推進課のウェブページからダウンロードもできます。 ●②で発表が確定していない場合でも応募することができます。その旨をAに記入のうえ、必ず募集期間内にAとBを提出してください。また、実際に発表するかどうかが決まり次第、速やかに連絡してください。 8. 採択後の注意事項 報告書と経費の領収書を帰国後2週間以内に提出してもらいます。未提出の書類があった場合は採択を取り消す可能性があります。 問い合わせ先：研究推進課 https://www.hoshikawa-u.ac.jp/research.html 電話：061-625-9398 Eメール：graduate-research@hoshikawa.ac.jp 19
p_question-4900.en: Hoshikawa University Application Guidelines for FY2022 “Global Research Activity Grant” 1. Eligibility (applicants must meet all of the following) Enrolled in a Hoshikawa University graduate program in FY2022 and not selected for this grant in FY2021 2. Eligible research activities (activities falling under either ① or ② below) ① Conduct field research overseas with a travel period of 10 days or more. ② Give a research presentation at an overseas academic meeting, symposium, etc. (no restriction on travel period). 3. Implementation period for eligible activities For both ① and ②, activities must take place between August 1, 2022 and March 31, 2023. 4. Grant amount, etc. The maximum grant per person is 200,000 yen. Each applicant may submit only one application. See the separate sheet for details of eligible expense items. 5. Selection Document screening 6. Schedule Application period: May 10 (Tue), 2022 – June 10 (Fri), 2022 Notification of results: Mid-July 2022 7. Application procedure Submit the following A–C together to the Office for Research Promotion. A Application form (Form 1) B Letter of recommendation (Form 2) ...Prepared by the academic advisor and submitted by the student. Please request preparation well in advance. C Copy of presentation application (for ② only) ...If the application opens after the grant application period, submit it as soon as it is ready. ● The forms for A and B will be distributed at the Office for Research Promotion from April 26 through the last day of the application period. They can also be downloaded from the office’s webpage. ● You may apply even if your presentation for ② has not been finalized. Indicate this on A, and be sure to submit A and B within the application period. Also, contact us promptly once it is decided whether you will actually present. 8. Notes after selection You must submit a report and receipts for expenses within two weeks after returning to Japan. If any documents are not submitted, selection may be revoked. Contact: Office for Research Promotion https://www.hoshikawa-u.ac.jp/research.html Tel: 061-625-9398 Email: graduate-research@hoshikawa.ac.jp 19

questions.stem: チームの新しい監督が決まった。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 3: かんとく is correct. Question Translation "チームの新しい監督が決まった。" → "A new manager for the team has been decided." Explanation The kanji "監督" is read as かんとく, which means *manager*, *director*, or *supervisor*. This is the standard and correct reading of the kanji in the context of sports or film, referring to someone in charge. Other Options 1. かんどく – Not a standard reading for 監督, and there is no relevant meaning. 2. がんどく – Incorrect and does not apply to 監督. 4. がんとく – Incorrect and commonly not a reading for 監督. Only かんとく matches the kanji 監督 appropriately in this sentence.

questions.stem: これは、外国語から派生した言葉です。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 2: はせい is correct. Question Translation "これは、外国語から派生した言葉です。" → "This is a word derived from a foreign language." Explanation The kanji "派生" is read as はせい, which means *derivation* or *derived*. This is the correct reading of the kanji when discussing something like a word derived from another language. Other Options 1. はしょう – Not a standard reading of 派生. 3. はっしょう – Implies a different meaning like *birth* or *origin* (kanji: 発祥), not 派生. 4. はっせい – Implies *occurrence* or *outbreak* (kanji: 発生), which does not fit 派生. Only はせい accurately matches the kanji 派生 in this context.

questions.stem: 文字が透けている。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 4: すけて is correct. Question Translation "文字が透けている。" → "The characters are showing through." Explanation The kanji "透けて" is read as すけて, which means *to show through* or *to be transparent*. This is the correct reading based on the kanji in the sentence. Other Options 1. ぼけて – Means *to fade* or *to be blurred* (kanji: 暈けて), which does not fit here. 2. かけて – Means *to hang* or *to be missing* (kanji: 掛けて or 欠けて), not appropriate here. 3. ぬけて – Means *to come out* or *to be omitted* (kanji: 抜けて), and does not match 透けて. Only すけて fits the kanji 透けて which means "to be transparent" in this context.

questions.stem: この村は湖の恩恵を受けている。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 1: おんけい is correct. Question Translation "この村は湖の恩恵を受けている。" → "This village receives the blessings of the lake." Explanation The kanji "恩恵" is read as おんけい, which means *blessing* or *benefit*. This is the correct reading based on the kanji in the sentence and the context. Other Options 2. おんえい – Not a standard reading of 恩恵. 3. いんけい – Not relevant, and does not match the reading for 恩恵. 4. いんえい – Means *shadow* or *shade* (kanji: 陰影), and does not match 恩恵. Only おんけい matches the kanji 恩恵 in this context.

questions.stem: 厳しい練習に臨む選手たちの姿を目にした。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 4: のぞむ is correct. Question Translation "厳しい練習に臨む選手たちの姿を目にした。" → "I saw the athletes facing the tough practice." Explanation The kanji "臨む" is read as のぞむ, which means *to face*, *to attend*, or *to confront*. This reading fits the context of athletes facing a challenging practice. Other Options 1. いどむ – Means *to challenge* (kanji: 挑む), which doesn’t fit here. 2. はげむ – Means *to strive* or *to be diligent* (kanji: 励む), not the correct kanji. 3. なやむ – Means *to worry* or *to be troubled* (kanji: 悩む), unrelated to the context. Only のぞむ correctly matches the kanji 臨む in this sentence.

questions.stem: 調査データは、地球の温暖化を如実に示した。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 3: にょじつに is correct. Question Translation "調査データは、地球の温暖化を如実に示した。" → "The survey data clearly showed global warming." Explanation The kanji "如実" is read as にょじつ, which means *realistically* or *clearly* depicting something as it truly is. This is the correct reading because it fits the context of the sentence, showing evidence or a clear presentation of data. Other Options 1. じょじつに – Not a standard reading of 如実. 2. じょうじつに – Incorrect reading, does not apply here. 4. にょうじつに – This reading does not align with the kanji 如実. Only にょじつに properly matches the kanji 如実 in this context.

questions.stem: 被害者について報道するときは、仮名を用いてプライバシーを（ ）している。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 1: 保護 (ほご) is correct. It means "protection" or "safeguarding." Question Translation When reporting about victims, pseudonyms are used to protect their privacy. Explanation The sentence discusses the practice in media of using pseudonyms for victims to maintain their privacy. "保護" (ほご) means "protection" or "safeguarding," which makes it the perfect choice here as it relates directly to protecting the privacy of individuals. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 2: 防御 (ぼうぎょ) means "defense" or "protection against attacks," generally used in a physical or strategic context, not suitable for discussing privacy. - Answer 3: 警備 (けいび) refers to "security" or "guarding," generally in the context of physical or location security, not applicable to abstract concepts like privacy. - Answer 4: 支援 (しえん) means "support" or "assistance." While it could relate to providing help, it does not specifically imply protection of privacy. Grammar/Vocabulary Note The term "保護" is commonly used in contexts involving safeguarding rights or personal information, such as "環境保護" (environmental protection) and "個人情報保護" (protection of personal information). Understanding these contexts helps in discerning the most appropriate choice in nuanced situations.

questions.stem: この模型は、実際にあった建物を20分の1の大きさで（ ）再現した物だ。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 3: 忠実に is correct. It means "faithfully" or "accurately." Question Translation This model is a representation that faithfully reproduces the building at one-twentieth of its actual size. Explanation The context of this sentence involves recreating a model of a building. The term 忠実に (ちゅうじつに) is used to indicate that the model is an accurate, faithful representation of the original building. This fits perfectly as the sentence describes replicating the actual structure while maintaining its true details, albeit on a smaller scale. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: 従順に (じゅうじゅんに) means "obediently" or "submissively." This doesn't fit the context, as it describes behavior or manner, not how something is reproduced or replicated. - Answer 2: 厳正に (げんせいに) means "strictly" or "rigorously." While it implies precision, it isn't commonly used to describe visual or physical replication. - Answer 4: 均等に (きんとうに) means "equally" or "uniformly." This term pertains to distribution or balance rather than accuracy or fidelity to an original model. Grammar/Vocabulary Note 忠実に is a key adverb used to describe how closely something matches the original, often used in contexts involving reproductions, adaptations, or translations to convey accuracy or faithfulness to the source material. Recognizing such adverbs is crucial for correctly interpreting sentences where precise replication or exact matching is intended.

questions.stem: 川の水が澄んでいてきれいだったので、両手で（ ）、顔を洗った。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 2: すくって is correct. It means "scoop up." Question Translation The water in the river was clear and beautiful, so I scooped it up with both hands and washed my face. Explanation The context of the sentence involves using both hands to gather water from a river to wash one's face. The verb "すくって" (掬って) perfectly fits this context as it means "to scoop up," which describes the action of gathering water with one's hands. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: めくって (捲って) means "to turn over" or "to flip" (like flipping a page). It doesn’t fit the action of gathering water. - Answer 3: はさんで (挟んで) means "to pinch" or "to insert between." It is not suitable for describing how water is gathered by hand. - Answer 4: つまんで (つまんで) means "to pinch" or "to pick up (with fingers)," which is generally used for small items, not for scooping water. Grammar/Vocabulary Note Understanding verbs related to actions involving hands is crucial here. In Japanese, verbs often precisely describe the manner of an action, especially when involving physical actions like gathering or picking up items. "すくう" (掬う) specifically refers to a motion suitable for scooping water, highlighting the importance of recognizing verb specificity and context in Japanese.

questions.stem: 必要な資金を調達した。

questions.stem: 山田さんは温和な性格だ。

questions.stem: おすすめのスポットを教えてください。

Explanation The word "スポット" in Japanese is a loanword from English, meaning "spot" or "place," particularly used to refer to locations of interest or recommended places to visit. In the context of the request for "おすすめのスポット," it refers to a recommended location or site. Therefore, the correct paraphrase is 場所, which means "place" or "location," fitting the context of discussing a spot or area to visit. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 2: 商品 (しょうひん) means "product" or "goods." It does not fit the context, as the question is asking about a place, not an item or product. - Answer 3: 方法 (ほうほう) means "method" or "way." questions.stem: 各国のチームの実力は拮抗している。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 2: 差がない is correct. It means "there is no difference." Question Translation The skill levels of the teams from various countries are evenly matched. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: 差がある means "there is a difference." This contradicts the meaning of 拮抗, which implies equality or a balance in skill levels, not a difference. - Answer 3: 高くなっている means "getting higher" or "improving." While skill levels might improve, this phrase doesn't relate to the concept of being evenly matched but rather an increase in performance. - Answer 4: 低くなっている means "getting lower" or "declining." This also doesn't align with the meaning of 拮抗, which focuses on balance and equality, not a decrease or decline in skill. Grammar/Vocabulary Note 拮抗 (きっこう) is a term often used in competitive contexts, such as sports or debates, to describe situations where competing parties have reached a level of parity, and neither side has a definitive advantage over the other. Recognizing words that denote comparison or balance can aid in understanding nuanced sentences.

questions.stem: 住民からの風当たりが相当なものだった。

questions.stem: カメラは子供たちのあどけない表情を捉えていた。

questions.stem: 出荷
opts: 有名俳優の出荷によって、公演のチケットはすぐに完売した。
海外の需要が高まり、アメリカに新店舗の出荷が決定した。
好評により注文が殺到していて、商品の出荷が遅れている。
今回の展覧会では、絵画の出荷が大半を占めた。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 3 is correct. Word Translation 出荷（しゅっか） — shipment, dispatch of goods Correct Sentence Translation Due to its popularity, there are a flood of orders, so the shipment of goods is delayed. Explanation The word 出荷 is used to describe the action of shipping or dispatching products or goods. In this context, "商品の出荷" (shipment of goods) accurately captures the logistics aspect related to commerce and trade. Other Options ① 有名俳優の出荷によって、公演のチケットはすぐに完売した。 → The actor's shipment caused the show tickets to sell out quickly. Incorrect Usage The term "出荷" does not refer to people or events. A more appropriate word would be "出演" (appearance). ② 海外の需要が高まり、アメリカに新店舗の出荷が決定した。 → Due to high overseas demand, the shipment of a new store to America was decided. Awkward Usage While "出荷" might technically apply to artwork being sent, exhibitions focus on displays, not logistics. Use "展示" (exhibit) instead. Usage Note "出荷" is specifically linked to the physical dispatching of products in a commercial or logistical setting. It is not used for abstract or non-physical entities like people, stores, or emotional contexts.

questions.stem: 底力

questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 2 is correct. Explanation The word 底力 is used to describe hidden or underlying strength that becomes apparent in critical situations. The sentence describes someone whose potential or hidden capability becomes evident during important moments, which is a natural use of this term. Other Options ① 毎日体を運動しているので、腕の__底力__なら誰にも負けない。 → I exercise my body every day, so when it comes to latent strength in my arms, I won't lose to anyone. questions.stem: 絶大
opts: 絶大な開発によって、町の風景がすっかり変わった。
世界を旅して絶大な山の姿を写真に収めた。

あの歌手は若い人たちに絶大な人気がある。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 4 is correct. Word Translation 絶大（ぜつだい） — tremendous, immense, huge Correct Sentence Translation That singer has immense popularity among young people. Explanation The word 絶大 is used to describe something that is large in scale or magnitude, often in terms of influence or popularity. "絶大な人気" (immense popularity) is a natural combination because it describes a high degree or level of popularity, commonly used in contexts of influence or admiration. Other Options ① __絶大__な開発によって、町の風景がすっかり変わった。 → Due to tremendous development, the town's landscape completely changed. Awkward Usage While "絶大" suggests a large magnitude, "絶大な開発" sounds unnatural because "development" is typically qualified by more specific terms like "大規模" (large-scale). ② 世界を旅して__絶大__な山の姿を写真に収めた。 → I traveled the world and captured images of tremendous mountains. Incorrect Usage "絶大" is not used for physical objects like mountains. Incorrect Usage "絶大" is not typically used to describe the size or degree of a fish. Suitable descriptors here might be "巨大" (huge) or "大きな" (big). Usage Note "絶大" is most effective when used to describe non-physical concepts such as influence, popularity, and power. It highlights extremeness in a metaphorical sense, rather than literal size.

questions.stem: 手痛い

questions.stem: 誘致

questions.stem: はっきりと覚えていないが、この町には（ ）来たことがある。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 3: いつか is correct. It means "someday" or "at some point (in the past or future)." Question Translation I don't remember clearly, but I have been to this town before at some point. Explanation The sentence is about a past event where the speaker has a vague memory of visiting the town. The word いつか (itsuka) appropriately expresses an indefinite time in the past in this context. It signifies that the speaker does remember visiting the town, but cannot pinpoint exactly when. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: また means "again" or "once more." It indicates repetition or a future occurrence and does not fit the context of a vague past memory. - Answer 2: ついに means "finally" or "at last." It implies the culmination of a long process and is used for expected outcomes, not fitting here since the context isn't about reaching a planned goal or expected event. - Answer 4: まもなく means "soon" or "shortly." It refers to an upcoming event and doesn’t mesh with the expression of an unclear past event. Grammar/Vocabulary Note The adverb いつか is often used to indicate an unspecified point in time, making it versatile for both past and future contexts. Understanding these time-related adverbs is crucial for interpreting and conveying nuances in time-specific statements in Japanese.

questions.stem: （ホームページで） 体育館は老朽化のため、本年3月15日（ ）閉館しました。長年のご利用、ありがとうございました。

Question Translation (The message on the homepage reads:) "Due to aging, the gymnasium has been closed as of March 15th of this year. Thank you for your many years of utilization." Explanation Answer 3, "をもって," is the correct choice because it marks a specific time when an event takes place, often used in formal announcements to indicate the closure or completion of an event or service. In this context, it signifies the exact point in time when the gymnasium closure occurred. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: につき generally means "due to" or "because of." While it might initially seem like it could work due to its relevance to the reasoning for closure (老朽化のため), it does not properly fit the sentence structure intended to mark the specific date of closure. - Answer 2: に至るまで means "up until" or "to," often used to describe a range or up to a certain point. The sentence indicates a specific ending and not a duration, making this choice inappropriate. - Answer 4: を皮切りに means "starting with" or "beginning with." It is used to describe the beginning of a series of events, which does not fit the context of this sentence describing the terminal act of closure. Grammar/Vocabulary Note The phrase "をもって" is a formal expression often used in written or official announcements to mark a specific date or event as definitive. It is important to recognize expre

questions.stem: 高校に入学して、特に入りたいクラブもなかったので、友人に（ ）、テニス部に入った。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 1: 誘われるまま is correct. It means "as I was invited" or "as I was led." Question Translation After entering high school, I didn't have any particular club I wanted to join, so I joined the tennis club as I was invited by a friend. Explanation The phrase 誘われるまま is used to express doing something as one is influenced or led by an external force, without much decision-making or resistance. In this sentence, it indicates that the speaker joined the tennis club simply because they were invited by their friend, without having a strong preference themselves. This matches the context where the speaker did not have a particular club in mind. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 2: 誘われようが suggests a conditional form resembling "even if invited." It implies a condition or hypothetical scenario that doesn't match the context of actually joining the club due to being invited. - Answer 3: 誘われた上に means "on top of being invited." It would indicate an additional reason or consequence, which the sentence does not suggest; the context is not about multiple factors. - Answer 4: 誘われない限り means "unless invited." This is incorrect as it implies a condition that opposes what actually happened — the speaker did join due to an invitation. Grammar/Vocabulary Note The expression まま often conveys a sense of doing something in the manner of a preceding state or action. In 誘われるまま, it denotes an action taken due to someone else's influence, emphasizing a passive or non-deliberate decis

questions.stem: （ホームページで）当店では、商品に欠陥がある（ ）、返品及び交換はお受けできませんので、あらかじめご了承ください。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 4: 場合を除いて is correct. It means "except in the case." Question Translation (On the homepage) At our store, except in the case of a defect in the product, we cannot accept returns or exchanges, so please understand this in advance. Explanation The sentence provides an exception to the store's policy on returns and exchanges. The phrase 場合を除いて (ばあいをのぞいて) means "except in the case" and indicates there is one specific condition (a defect in the product) where the normal rule (no returns or exchanges) does not apply. This expression is necessary here to explain that returns are only possible under specific circumstances (defective products). Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: 点に加えて (てんにくわえて) means "in addition to the point." This suggests adding conditions rather than specifying an exception, which does not suit the context of excluding returns except for certain cases. - Answer 2: 点を除いて (てんをのぞいて) means "except for the point." While it has a similar structure to the correct answer, "点" (point) is too vague and doesn’t specify a scenario or condition like "場合" (case) does, making it unsuitable for this context. - Answer 3: 場合に加えて (ばあいにくわえて) means "in addition to the case." This implies supplementing the current terms with more conditions, which is opposite to providing an exception. Grammar/Vocabulary Note The expression "場合を除いて" is a common way to specify exceptions in rules or policies. It's a useful phrase to know for understanding nuanced exception

questions.stem: （取引先へのメールで）来週の打ち合わせの資料をお送りします。お忙しいとは（ ）が、ご確認いただければ幸いです。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 2: 存じます is correct. It means "I suppose" or "I am aware," often used in a humble context. Question Translation (In an email to a client) I will send you the materials for next week's meeting. Although I am aware that you are busy, I would be grateful if you could check them. Explanation In formal or business Japanese, it's important to use humble language when talking about one's own actions or assumptions involving a client or customer. The phrase "お忙しいとは存じますが" conveys humility and respect, recognizing the recipient's likely busy schedule while politely asking them to review the materials. 存じます (ぞんじます) is a humble form of 知る, used to discuss one's own knowledge or suppositions. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: いたします is a humble form of する, meaning "to do," often used to describe one's own actions, but it doesn't make sense in the middle of this sentence since it would disrupt the logical structure of expressing awareness. - Answer 3: いらっしゃいます is the honorific form of "to be" or "to go," used to describe the actions or states of others, particularly with respect, but it doesn't fit this context where the speaker expresses their own knowledge or assumption. - Answer 4: 申し上げます is a humble form of 言う, meaning "to say," which is used to inform the listener respectfully about what the speaker is saying, not to express knowledge or awareness. Grammar/Vocabulary Note The phrase "～とは存じますが," is a common way to express understanding or assumptions about

questions.stem: 学生時代はずっとサッカーをしていたのだが、社会人に（ ）、運動する機会がすっかり減ってしまった。
opts: なってからであっても
なってからというもの
なったばかりだとして
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 3: なってからというもの is correct. It means "ever since becoming." Question Translation During my student days, I played soccer all the time, but ever since becoming a working adult, my opportunities to exercise have drastically decreased. Explanation The phrase なってからというもの implies a change that has occurred and continues to have an ongoing effect or condition. In this context, it means that the speaker's shift from student to working adult has led to a lasting decrease in exercise opportunities, which continues to be the case from the point of this change onward. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: なってからであっても translates to "even after having become." This phrase would imply that even after becoming a working adult, the speaker still exercised, which contradicts the sentence saying that opportunities to exercise have decreased. - Answer 2: なったばかりなのか translates to "just became, or just after becoming." This expression captures a temporal closeness to the change but lacks the nuance of an ongoing condition, unsuitable for describing a long-term decrease in exercise. - Answer 4: なったばかりだとして translates to "on the assumption that one just became." This implies a hypothetical scenario or an assumption, not fitting for a factual description of continued reduced exercise opportunities following a real change in status. Grammar/Vocabulary Note The construction "～というもの" is often used to express a significant change or new situation that leads to a continuous effect o

questions.stem: 自転車の鍵が見当たらないと思ったら、昨日はいていたズボンのポケットに（ ）。

questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 2: 入れっぱなしだった is correct. It means "was left inside" or "was left as is." Question Translation When I thought I couldn't find the bicycle key, it was left in the pocket of the pants I wore yesterday. Explanation The sentence describes the situation where the speaker realizes where the missing bicycle key was found. The phrase 入れっぱなしだった (いれっぱなしだった) implies that the key was placed in the pocket and remained there without being removed. This usage fits the context because it explains the oversight of forgetting to take the key out of the pocket. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: 入れたつもり means "I thought I put it in." While this could fit the scenario initially, it does not convey the idea that the key was left in the pocket, which is necessary for the complete explanation of the situation. - Answer 3: 入れっこなかった is a grammatically incorrect form and would not be used in this context. It suggests the impossibility of putting it in, which does not relate to the context where the key was indeed placed in the pocket. - Answer 4: 入れるべきではなかった means "should not have put it in," but this suggests a sense of regret or a wrong action, which doesn't describe merely losing track of where the key was. Grammar/Vocabulary Note The pattern っぱなし indicates that something has been left in a particular state. In this context, 入れっぱなし implies the key was left in the pocket without being removed. This is a useful pattern to recognize, especially in situations where items ar

questions.stem: A「ねえねえ、北市にできた動物園、いったんでしょう？あそこ、相当広いらしいね」B「（ ）、1日じゃ全部回れなかったよ」
opts: 広くないはずなんだよ
広いなんてことはないよ
広くないわけじゃないんだよ
広いなんてもんじゃないよ
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 4: 広いなんてもんじゃないよ is correct. It means "It's not just wide; it's way more than that." Question Translation A: "Hey, you've been to the zoo that opened in Kitashi, right? I heard it's pretty spacious." B: "It's not just spacious; we couldn't cover it all in one day." Explanation The question revolves around responding to the statement that the zoo is "相当広い" (pretty spacious). The expression "広いなんてもんじゃないよ" is an emphatic way to say it is not simply "wide" but far exceeds that expectation. It suggests that the zoo's size is beyond ordinary spaciousness, aligning with the follow-up remark about not covering it all in one day. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: 広くないはずなんだよ translates to "It shouldn't be wide," which contradicts the context. The speaker agrees that the zoo is more spacious than the listener assumed. - Answer 2: 広いなんてことはないよ means "It's not wide at all," directly opposing both the information given by speaker A and the content of speaker B’s experience. - Answer 3: 広くないわけじゃないんだよ translates to "It's not that it's not wide." This is a double negative, which tentatively agrees but lacks the emphatic strength needed in this context. Grammar/Vocabulary Note The phrase "なんてもんじゃない" is an idiomatic expression used to stress that something is more extraordinary or extreme than described. Recognizing such expressions helps in understanding exaggerated or intensified agreement or sentiment in speech.

questions.stem: 私は昔からバイクが大好きだ。小さい頃は、よく父のバイクの後ろに（ ）。
opts: 乗りたがったところだ
乗って欲しがったものだ
乗って欲しがったところだ
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 1: 乗りたがったものだ is correct. It means "used to want to ride." Question Translation I have loved motorcycles since I was young. When I was little, I often wanted to ride on the back of my father's motorcycle. Explanation The sentence is recalling a past habitual action or feeling. The phrase "乗りたがったものだ" utilizes "〜たがる," which indicates someone else's desire and "ものだ," which is used to describe things that used to happen regularly in the past. Here, "乗りたがったものだ" effectively conveys the speaker's past desire as a child to ride on the motorcycle regularly. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 2: 乗りたがったところだ implies a specific moment in time when the speaker wanted to ride. "ところだ" suggests a temporal point, unsuitable to express a habitual action or feeling in the past. - Answer 3: 乗って欲しがったものだ means "wanted someone else to ride," which shifts the subject of desire incorrectly from the speaker to someone else and fails to express habitual past desire. - Answer 4: 乗って欲しがったところだ similarly suggests the speaker wanted someone else to ride and focuses on a specific moment. The phrase "ところだ" is not suitable for expressing past habitual action or feeling. Grammar/Vocabulary Note "〜たがる" is used in the third person to express desire. The "ものだ" form is important for expressing habitual actions or feelings in the past, offering a sense of nostalgia or fondness. Recognizing the use of "たがる" for indicating someone else’s desire and the habitual nature of "ものだ" helps in select

questions.stem: 秋の初めでのこの時期は、真夏の戻った ______ ★ ______ ______ 、気温の差が大きくて体調を崩しやすいので、注意が必要だ。
questions.expl: Answer (★ Position) Answer 2: 日もあれば (★ is in the 2nd slot) Correct Order 4 → 2 → 3 → 1 かのような → 日もあれば → ひんやりとした → 日もあり Chunk-by-Chunk Breakdown ④ かのような — “as if …”; na-adjectival modifier forming a simile. It attaches to 「真夏が戻った」 to modify 日. ② 日もあれば — “there are days (when) …”; first half of the parallel-contrast pattern Xもあれば、Yもある. ③ ひんやりとした — “chilly, crisp”; adjectival phrase modifying 日. ① 日もあり — “there are also days …”; balances the pattern with the second half Yもあり. Reconstructed Sentence 秋の初めのこの時期は、真夏が戻ったかのような日もあれば、ひんやりとした日もあり、気温の差が大きくて体調を崩しやすいので、注意が必要だ。 → At this time in early autumn, some days feel as if midsummer has returned, while others are chilly, so the temperature swings are large and it’s easy to get sick; be careful. Why This Order Works - 「かのような」 must directly modify 日 after the clause 「真夏が戻った」. - The construction 「X日もあれば、Y日もあり」 cleanly contrasts two kinds of days. - This contrast sets up the reason for large temperature differences and the warning.

questions.stem: 歴史的価値が高いとされる旧日本小学校の校舎を始めて見たが、とても ______ ______ ★ ______ 驚いた。
questions.expl: Answer (★ Position) Answer 4: 思えないほどの Correct Order 1 → 3 → 4 → 2 100年前に → 建てられたものとは → 思えないほどの → 現代的なデザインに Chunk-by-Chunk Breakdown ① 100年前に — “100 years ago”; time adverbial modifying 建てられた ③ 建てられたものとは — “(as) something that was built”; とは sets up the pattern 〜とは思えない (“can’t think it is ~”) ④ 思えないほどの — “so ~ that one can’t think (it is) …”; ほど marks degree; の makes it an adnominal modifier for the following noun ② 現代的なデザインに — “at/with its modern design”; に marks the object of 驚く (“be surprised at”) Reconstructed Sentence 歴史的価値が高いとされる旧日本小学校の校舎を初めて見たが、とても100年前に建てられたものとは思えないほどの現代的なデザインに驚いた。 → I saw the former Japan Elementary School building, said to have high historical value, for the first time, and I was very surprised at its modern design—so modern that you wouldn’t think it was built 100 years ago. Why This Order Works - 100年前に modifies 建てられた, forming the relative clause “built 100 years ago.” - 〜ものとは思えないほどの creates “so ~ that you can’t think it’s something built 100 years ago,” which then modifies 現代的なデザイン. - に with 現代的なデザイン pairs naturally with 驚いた (“be surprised at”).

questions.stem: くしゃみや鼻水といった症状が現れる花粉症は、風邪と ______ ______ ★ ______ ケースもあるそうだ。
opts: 自分は花粉症だという
questions.expl: Answer (★ Position) Answer 2: 自分は花粉症だという (★ is third) Correct Order 3 → 4 → 2 → 1 症状が似ている → こともあって → 自分は花粉症だという → 自覚がない Chunk-by-Chunk Breakdown ③ 症状が似ている — “the symptoms are similar”; describes similarity “to a cold” with 風邪と ④ こともあって — “partly because (of the fact that) …”; nominalises ③ and gives a contributing reason ② 自分は花粉症だという — “that one has hay fever”; quotative clause modifying 自覚 ① 自覚がない — “have no awareness”; predicates “自覚,” forming “自覚がないケース” Reconstructed Sentence くしゃみや鼻水といった症状が現れる花粉症は、風邪と症状が似ていることもあって、自分は花粉症だという自覚がないケースもあるそうだ。 → For hay fever, which causes symptoms like sneezing and a runny nose, partly because the symptoms are similar to a cold, there are cases where people aren’t aware that they have hay fever. Why This Order Works - 症状が似ている + こともあって sets up a contributing reason (“partly because the symptoms resemble a cold”). - 自分は花粉症だという modifies 自覚, creating “awareness that one has hay fever.” - 自覚がない finishes the noun phrase “自覚がないケース,” fitting naturally before ケース. - Star position is third: the ★ chunk 自分は花粉症だという must come before 自覚がない to modify 自覚.

questions.stem: 高校卒業後の進路について両親に反対されたが、誰に何と ______ ______ ★ ______ 思う。
questions.expl: Answer (★ Position) Answer 1: 思える道を（★ is 3rd position: ___ ___ ★ ___） Correct Order 3 → 4 → 1 → 2 言われようとも → 自分がこれだと → 思える道を → 信じて進みたいと Chunk-by-Chunk Breakdown ③ 言われようとも — “even if (I’m) told / no matter what (people) say”; concessive pattern ようとも pairing with 誰に何と ④ 自分がこれだと — “that this is it for me”; quotative と, setting the content of 思える to modify 道 ① 思える道を — “a path I can feel/think ‘this is it’ about”; noun phrase marked by を as the object ② 信じて進みたいと — “(I) want to believe (in it) and move forward,” with quotative と linking to final 思う Reconstructed Sentence 高校卒業後の進路について両親に反対されたが、誰に何と言われようとも、自分がこれだと思える道を信じて進みたいと思う。 → Although my parents opposed my post–high school path, no matter who says what, I want to believe in the path I feel is right for me and move forward. Why This Order Works 1) 誰に何と pairs naturally with 言われようとも to form “no matter who says what.” 2) 自分がこれだと + 思える creates a relative clause modifying 道. 3) 思える道を supplies the object for 信じて進みたい. 4) 信じて進みたいと connects to the sentence-final 思う.

questions.stem: 企業の海外進出が成功するかどうかは、 ______ ______ ★ ______ といってもいいだろう。
opts: 優秀な人材を確保できる
その国の事情をよく知る
questions.expl: Answer (★ Position) Answer 3: か否か (★ is third) Correct Order 4 → 1 → 3 → 2 その国の事情をよく知る → 優秀な人材を確保できる → か否か → にかかっている Chunk-by-Chunk Breakdown ④ その国の事情をよく知る — relative clause “who/that know(s) the circumstances of that country well”; modifies 人材 ① 優秀な人材を確保できる — “can secure excellent personnel”; predicate that the clause revolves around ③ か否か — “whether or not”; nominalizes the preceding clause into an embedded question ② にかかっている — “depends on”; takes a nominalized phrase with に Reconstructed Sentence 企業の海外進出が成功するかどうかは、その国の事情をよく知る優秀な人材を確保できるか否かにかかっている、といってもいいだろう。 → As for whether a company’s overseas expansion will succeed, one could say it depends on whether they can secure excellent personnel who know the local situation well. Why This Order Works - その国の事情をよく知る modifies 優秀な人材, forming “excellent personnel who know the country’s situation well.” - か否か nominalizes “(can) secure …” into “whether [they] can secure …,” which can be the object of に. - にかかっている expresses “depends on,” attaching to the 〜か否か clause. - ★ is third because か否か must follow the verb phrase it nominalizes and precede にかかっている.

Correct Answer Answer 3: たしかに is correct. It means "indeed; certainly." Question Translation "These modifiers [indeed] stimulate our appetite and heighten our expectations. But, on closer thought, the substance of that differentiation is actually quite vague." Explanation The paragraph first concedes a positive effect of promotional modifiers before criticizing their vagueness. たしかに marks this concessive acknowledgment ("indeed … but …"). It naturally introduces a statement that will be contrasted in the next sentence with でも. Why Other Options Are Incorrect 1. むしろ — "rather; instead" → Suggests a replacement/contrast inside the same proposition, not a concessive lead-in to a following "but." Unnatural here. 2. かえって — "on the contrary; rather (with an opposite/unexpected result)" → Implies the effect is opposite to expectation. The author is not claiming the words *fail* to stimulate appetite; they concede that they do, then criticize later. 4. ようやく — "finally; at last" → Indicates passage of time or difficulty overcome; irrelevant to the logical flow about effects of modifiers. Grammar/Vocabulary Note "A（たしかに）…。でも、B…" is a common discourse pattern to concede A while preparing to present a contrasting point B.

Correct Answer Answer 4: そのためだ is correct. It means "because of that" or "due to that reason." Question Translation The sentence for blank [42] translates to: "In a highly consumer society where the development of truly substantial new products has become difficult, there’s a strong tendency to stimulate consumption with apparent novelty. Frequently changing product packaging or altering product names is also [42]". Explanation The passage discusses how superficial changes are made to products to attract consumers when substantial product improvements are scarce. The phrase そのためだ (sono tame da) logically connects the reasoning provided—such as changing packaging and product names as strategies for stimulating consumption through apparent novelty—back to the core theme of heightened consumerism and the lack of genuine product differentiation. Therefore, "due to that reason" fits the context as it completes the cause-effect relationship in the passage. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: それ以来だ means "since then." It implies a time sequence or continuity from a past event rather than establishing a cause for the current situation, making it illogical here. - Answer 2: それだけだ means "that’s all," which implies a limitation or conclusion, not explaining a cause for the actions mentioned. - Answer 3: そのままだ means "as it is" or "unchanged," which doesn’t match as the sentence is about changes being frequently made rather than things remaining unchanged. Grammar/Vocabulary Note

Correct Answer Answer 2: すら is correct. It means "even (the extreme case)." Question Translation "Such a society may even change our linguistic sensibilities." Explanation すら emphasizes an extreme example to highlight scope: 私たちの言語感覚すら変えてしまう = "may change even our language sense." This fits the author’s warning tone. Why Other Options Are Incorrect 1. が — subject marker → "…言語感覚が変えてしまう" would make "language sense" the agent that changes something else, which breaks the intended meaning and clashes with the topic は earlier. 3. なら — "if/as for" → Would yield an unfinished conditional or contrast (…言語感覚なら変えて…) that is ungrammatical/incoherent here. 4. でもって — colloquial "and then/with" → Casual, conjunctive slang unsuited to a formal column and grammatically odd in this slot. Grammar/Vocabulary Note "N すら" is a formal/emphatic variant of さえ, often used in writing to stress that even an extreme/unexpected item is included.

Correct Answer Answer 1: 使者のようでもある is correct. It means "are like messengers." Question Translation "Must we keep playing with empty signs while surrounded by proliferating make-believe newness? The modifiers lined up on menus are like messengers from the distant future of consumer society." Explanation The author metaphorically casts today’s menu modifiers as harbingers of where consumer society is heading. 〜のようでもある ("can even be seen as; are like") presents a tentative metaphor that matches the reflective tone. Why Other Options Are Incorrect 2. 使者とのことである — "it is said that (they are) messengers" → Reportive tone (とのこと) is out of place and too assertive about a literal identity. 3. 使者だとは言えない — "cannot be said to be messengers" → Denies the metaphor, contradicting the intended warning/harbinger nuance. 4. 使者にもなり得まい — "would not even become messengers" → Strong negation of possibility; again opposite to the author’s suggestion. Grammar/Vocabulary Note "N のようでもある" softens a comparison, often used in essays to float an interpretive metaphor without asserting it as fact.

questions.stem: 「嫌い」の感情について、筆者の考えに合うのはどれか。
opts: 否定的で強い感情であり、振り回されてもしかたがない。
なくせない感情であり、受け入れたほうがいい。
認めることで、その感情を抑えることができる。
理由を考えれば、振り回されることはない。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 2 is correct. It means "It’s an emotion that cannot be eliminated, so it’s better to accept it." Question Translation About the emotion "dislike (嫌い)," which statement matches the author’s view? Explanation The passage says “嫌い” is a negative feeling but also a valuable, important function that cannot be eradicated (根絶できません). The first step to avoid being swayed is to acknowledge/accept that feeling (嫌いを認めること). Therefore, accepting an unerasable emotion fits the author’s message. Why Other Options Are Incorrect 1. 否定的で強い感情であり、振り回されてもしかたがない。 — “It’s a negative, strong emotion, so it’s unavoidable to be tossed around by it.” → The author says the first step to *avoid* being swayed is to acknowledge it; not that being swayed is inevitable. 3. 認めることで、その感情を抑えることができる。 — “By acknowledging it, you can suppress that emotion.” → The text does not claim suppression. It says acknowledging helps you not be controlled by it, and that the emotion itself is important/beneficial. 4. 理由を考えれば、振り回されることはない。 — “If you think about the reasons, you won’t be swayed.” → No such claim. The recommended step is to recognize/accept the feeling, not necessarily to analyze reasons. questions.stem: 品質にこだわる戦略とは、どういうことか。
opts: 品質に見合った価格であると主張する。

questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 1 is correct. It means “Assert that the price is commensurate with the quality.” Question Translation What does a strategy that emphasizes quality (品質にこだわる) mean? Explanation The passage says even with a quality-first strategy, you still work to keep prices down; however, quality limits how low you can go. Even if the product is pricier than competitors, it has value equal to that price, and you ask customers to accept the difference. This is exactly “a price that matches the quality.” Why Other Options Are Incorrect 2. 品質の良さと価格の低さを主張する。 — “Claim both high quality and low price.” → The text says you *try* to lower price, but cannot make it very cheap if you insist on quality. 3. 価格に合わせた品質であると主張する。 — “Claim the quality is adjusted to the price.” → Opposite logic. The strategy prioritizes quality first, then justifies price, not the other way around. 4. 価格よりも、品質の良さを主張する。 — “Emphasize quality over price.” → Partly true in spirit, but incomplete: the passage stresses both efforts to control price and justifying a higher price by value, i.e., price commensurate with quality, not simply ignoring price. Grammar/Vocabulary Note - 〜に見合う: “be commensurate with, match (value/price).” - こだわる: “insist on / be particular about.”

This aligns with Answer 1, which focuses on the need to engage with these questions and reflect on one's daily life. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 2: "Parents sometimes learn the real meaning of life from their children." questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 4 is correct. It means that A suggests you don't need to do anything special, and B suggests accepting the situation and thinking about what should be done next. Question Translation What do A and B mention is a good way to handle a slump? Explanation In passage A, it mentions that during a slump, one should not unnecessarily struggle to overcome it; instead, it’s important to accept the current state and continue doing what you normally do, suggesting there's no need for special actions. For passage B, it advises against comparing to past successes and suggests acknowledging the current situation and focusing on 'what to do next,' promoting a more proactive and reflective approach. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: While A suggests maintaining the status quo until things improve, B emphasizes future planning rather than just waiting for improvement. - Answer 2: A doesn't suggest actively seeking ways to improve outcomes during a slump; rather, it recommends continuing as is. B stresses accepting the present and planning, not directly seeking methods for immediate success. - Answer 3: This option misrepresents B’s stance. B does not suggest leaving everything to nature; it recommends accepting the present and planning for future actions. Grammar/Vocabulary Note The phrase "波に身を任せる" (なみにみをまかせる) in passage A is an idiom suggesting one should let things take their natural course, similar to the English expression 'go with the flow.' questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 3: 自分という人間を意識することになるから is correct. It means "Because it will lead to becoming aware of oneself as a human being." Question Translation Although it may be just one model, it is a good thing to attempt to be individualistic in adolescence. Even if it includes failing or avoiding being individualistic, thinking about what individuality means to you is a good thing. This is proof of your existence as yourself. Explanation The passage discusses the process of becoming aware of oneself, and how trying to be individualistic during adolescence, even if it involves failures or conformity to a stereotype, is a valuable part of self-awareness and establishing one's own identity. The statement that "it is a good thing" refers to the process of self-discovery and appreciating one's individuality in the broader sense of understanding oneself, rather than simply conforming or rebelling. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: 個性的である必要はないと気づくから. This means "because you realize there is no need to be individualistic." This does not capture the essence of the passage, which focuses on the journey toward self-awareness and embracing individuality, not dismissing it altogether. - Answer 2: 挫折したり悩んだりする経験が個性を作るから. This means "because experiences of failure and worry create individuality." questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 4: 求めて得るものではなく、初めから持っているものだ is correct. It means "It's not something you acquire by seeking; it's something you have from the beginning." Question Translation What does the author think about individuality? Explanation The passage explains that individuality (個性) is inherent in every person and is not something that can be achieved simply by wanting to be unique or by fitting into a preconceived notion of "individualistic." The author emphasizes that true individuality is present in everyone from the start, and realizing one's true self is what makes someone genuinely individualistic. This aligns with Answer 4, which captures the essence that individuality is innate and not something to be pursued externally. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: 自分から社会とかかわることで、意識できるものだ ("It's something that is realized through involvement with society"). While the passage discusses the impact of societal interactions on expressing individuality, it specifically underlines that individuality is inherently present, not "realized" through social involvement alone. - Answer 2: 他人と比較しなければ、自然に発揮できるものだ ("It's something that can be naturally expressed if not compared to others"). Although the passage discusses not conforming to social molds, it emphasizes the pre-existing nature of individuality rather than it being hindered by or dependent on comparisons to others. - Answer 3: なりたい自分を意識すれば、自然に身につくものだ ("It's something naturally acquired if you are conscious of the self yo

questions.stem: 筆者が最も言いたいことは何か。
opts: 自分の個性を模索すべきだ。
自分自身でいることが大事だ。
ありのままの自分を出せなくてもいい。

questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 2: 自分自身でいることが大事だ. It means "It's important to be oneself." Question Translation What is the main thing the author wants to say? Explanation The passage emphasizes the importance of being true to oneself and recognizing one's individuality. The author discusses the societal pressure to fit into a mold of being "unique" and the struggles of adolescence in pursuing individuality. However, the author concludes that true individuality is inherently within everyone, and the key is to understand and embrace one's true self. Therefore, "It's important to be oneself" encapsulates the essence of the passage. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: 自分の個性を模索すべきだ ("One should explore one's individuality.") — Although exploring individuality is mentioned, the passage suggests that simply seeking individuality is not enough without self-awareness. The focus is more on being oneself rather than just exploring individuality. - Answer 3: ありのままの自分を出せなくてもいい ("It's okay not to reveal your true self.") — This contradicts the passage's conclusion, which stresses that understanding and accepting your true self is what makes one truly individualistic. - Answer 4: 型に縛られない生き方をしたほうがいい ("It's better to live independently of any mold.") — While this idea aligns with breaking societal molds, the main theme is deeper: embracing the authentic self, not just rejecting molds. questions.stem: 次の4人は、星川大学の大学院に在学中で、以下の研究活動を行いたいと思っている。「グローバル研究活動助成」に応募できるのは誰か。 名前 研究活動 渡航期間 この助成の採択歴 西田さん 海外のシンポジウムで講演を聞く 2022年8月24日から3日間 なし ダリアさん 海外に渡航し、資料の収集や調査を行う 2023年1月18日から3日間 なし ラウンさん 海外で研究発表を行う 2023年2月20日から5日間 2020年度にあり 山本さん 海外に渡航し、調査を行う 2022年8月20日から12日間 2021年度にあり
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 3 is correct. It means “Ms./Mr. Rawan (i.e., the person who will give an overseas presentation).” Question Translation Among the four graduate students, who can apply for the “Global Research Activity Grant”? Explanation Eligibility: (1) enrolled in 2022 and not selected in 2021; (2) activity must be either overseas investigation for ≥10 days or overseas research presentation (any duration); (3) conducted Aug 1, 2022–Mar 31, 2023. The candidate in option 3 (表の「ラウンさん」に相当) plans an overseas presentation for 5 days starting 2023/2/20, had a selection in 2020 (not 2021), so all conditions are satisfied. Why Other Options Are Incorrect 1. 西田さん。 — “Ms./Mr. Nishida.” → Attends overseas symposium to listen only; not a presentation and not a ≥10-day investigation. Fails condition (2). 2. グリアさん。 — “Ms./Mr. Gulia (intended: ダリアさん).” → Overseas investigation for 3 days (2023/1/18–). Condition (1) day-length requires 10 days or more; fails condition (2). 4. 山本さん。 — “Ms./Mr. Yamamoto.” → Overseas investigation for 12 days starting 2022/8/20 meets the ≥10-day rule and period, but has selection in 2021, which disqualifies under “2021年度に採択されていない者.” Vocabulary Note - 採択: selection/award (of a grant).

questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 2 is correct. It means “The required documents differ depending on whether the activity is a presentation or an investigation.” Question Translation Regarding the application, what must you keep in mind? Explanation In the procedures, A (application) and B (recommendation) are always required, while C (copy of presentation application) is required only for case ② (presentation). Thus, document requirements do differ by activity type. Why Other Options Are Incorrect 1. 指導教員から研究推進課窓口に提出してもらわなければならない書類がある。 — “There is a document that must be submitted to the office by the advisor.” → B (recommendation) is written by the advisor but submitted by the student. 3. 記入用紙は、5月10日までに研究推進課窓口に取りに行かなければならない。 — “You must pick up the forms at the office by May 10.” → Forms are available from 4/26 until the final day of the application period and can also be downloaded; no May 10 deadline to pick up. 4. 研究発表の場合、発表を行うことが確定していないと応募ができない。 — “For a presentation, you cannot apply unless the presentation is confirmed.” → You can apply even if not yet confirmed; note it on form A and submit A & B within the period, then notify once decided.
